title: Nesterenko Application Analysis

theorem.name: Nesterenko 1996
theorem.statement: pi, e^pi, Gamma(1/4) are algebraically independent over Q
corollaries: pi and e^pi are algebraically independent
For algebraic a != 0: pi and e^(pi*a) are algebraically independent
1 is not in Q-linear span of {pi, e^pi} alone (would require algebraic dependence)

case_A_pure_real_negative.description: Im from Log of negative real: Im = -pi or +pi

case_B_sin_of_pi_half.blocker: pi/2 not known to be in EML₁ Im-parts
case_B_sin_of_pi_half.if_it_were: exp(Re(x)) = 1 + arg(y) — transcendental = alg combo of 1 and pi

case_C_non_pi_multiples.blocker: Both Im(x) and sin(Im(x)) are transcendental; can't use Baker directly

partial_result.claim: If Im(EML₁) ⊆ Z*pi + Z*e^pi, then 1 ∉ Im(EML₁)
partial_result.proof: By Nesterenko, pi and e^pi algebraically independent, so 1 not in their Q-linear span
partial_result.gap: Containment Im(EML₁) ⊆ Z*pi + Z*e^pi is unproved

conclusion: Nesterenko is a useful tool ONCE the structural characterization of Im(EML₁) is known. Direct application is blocked by the fact that EML₁ Im-parts are themselves transcendental. Need: either Schanuel (to handle algebraic independence of {1, i}), or a direct structural proof that Im(EML₁) ⊆ Z·π.
next_session: S83: Gelfond-Schneider angle — treat tan(1) via algebraic power theory
